coredata.dc:description: Multiple trace heavy metal pollutants present a serious threat to the aquatic environment, and their coexistence also affected the detection efficiency. This work prepared Eu doped metal organic framework (MOF) complexed with S-containing carbon dots (CD(s)) exhibited dual-emission fluorescence. Its chelation-enhanced fluorescence with heavy metals replaced the original agglomerative fluorescence quenching of pure CD(s), which was utilized to construct a “turn on” fluorescence sensing probe. Based on the XPS, DFT and fluorescence lifetime results, the N, S functional groups on the surface of the materials could bind specifically to Hg2+ and Pb2+ with enhanced sensitivity. The limit of detection reached 76.67 nM for Hg2+ and 5.12 nM for Pb2+ within 2 min detection time. Furthermore, due to the different response mechanism of the two signal peaks to Hg2+ and Pb2+, a three-dimensional data model was developed for simultaneous detection in the co-existence system. The model had been successfully applied to actual Hg2+-Pb2+ containing water samples with recoveries ranged from 93.15 ∼ 105.08 %. The results provided a new strategy for simultaneous detection of trace environmental pollutants.

SEM images of (a, b) UiO-66-NH2, (c, d) UiO-66-NH2(Eu), (e, f) UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s).

(a,b) Fluorescence intensity change (FL) spectra of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) induced by the concentration of Hg2+ in the concentration range of 0–1 mg/L; (c) selectivity and (d) specificity of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s); (e,f) FL spectra of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) induced by Pb2+ concentration in the concentration range of 0–1 mg/L.

UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) and its fluorescence energy changes before and after detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+.
Fluorescence lifetimes of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s), UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s)@Hg and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s)@Pb.
Determination of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in real samples (coexistence system, all samples are sewage plant effluent treated with the Hg2+ and Pb2+ standard solution).

head.ce:title: Eu-doped carbon dots-MOF based turn-on fluorescent probe for trace Hg2+ and Pb2+ and construction of the simultaneous detection model

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Urban Construction, Nanjing Tech University, Puzhu Road 30, Nanjing 211816, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Urban Construction, Nanjing Tech University, Puzhu Road 30, Nanjing 211816, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multiple trace heavy metal pollutants present a serious threat to the aquatic environment, and their coexistence also affected the detection efficiency. This work prepared Eu doped metal organic framework (MOF) complexed with S-containing carbon dots (CD(s)) exhibited dual-emission fluorescence. Its chelation-enhanced fluorescence with heavy metals replaced the original agglomerative fluorescence quenching of pure CD(s), which was utilized to construct a “turn on” fluorescence sensing probe. Based on the XPS, DFT and fluorescence lifetime results, the N, S functional groups on the surface of the materials could bind specifically to Hg2+ and Pb2+ with enhanced sensitivity. The limit of detection reached 76.67 nM for Hg2+ and 5.12 nM for Pb2+ within 2 min detection time. Furthermore, due to the different response mechanism of the two signal peaks to Hg2+ and Pb2+, a three-dimensional data model was developed for simultaneous detection in the co-existence system. The model had been successfully applied to actual Hg2+-Pb2+ containing water samples with recoveries ranged from 93.15 ∼ 105.08 %. The results provided a new strategy for simultaneous detection of trace environmental pollutants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Many toxic heavy metal(HM) ions (Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, etc.) have persistent adverse effects on organisms even at very low concentration levels [1,2]. Therefore, efficient and clean detection of trace HM pollutants in the aqueous environment has become an important research topic in the field of environmental monitoring. Currently, the detection of HMs mainly relies on atomic absorption spectrometry(AAS) [3], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [4] and electrochemical analysis [5,6]. However, most of these methods require special equipment and complicated operation, and the detection results are not intuitive. In contrast, fluorescence sensing as a novel visual sensing technology, has received more attention because of its simplicity, low cost, good visibility and low detection limit [7–9]. Therefore, it is meaningful to develop new fluorescent sensing materials to realize rapid and intuitive quantitative detection of trace HMs in water.

To date, fluorescent materials such as organic fluorescent dyes [10,11], lanthanides [12,13] and luminescent nanomaterials have been developed for HM detection. Among them, metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have favorable spatial structure and diverse functional groups, exhibiting more excellent fluorescence sensing performance and detection ability [14,15]. Specifically, the large specific surface area and stereospecific spatial structure of MOFs not only enable them to load a variety of luminescent groups to achieve fluorescence modulation [16,17], but also utilize the fluorescence energy transfer of the ligands to achieve the fluorescence sensitization of the central metal [17]. For example, introducing lanthanides (Ln3+) and sensitizing rare metals by suitable ligands resulted in MOFs with multiple fluorescence emission peaks [18–20]. On this basis, Li et al [21] added Eu3+ to ZIF-8 to obtain a dual fluorescence emission composite for the detection of the anthrax biomarker dipyridyl acid. Peng et al [22] introduced Tb3+ into UiO-66(COOH)2 and synthesized fluorescent materials capable of detecting Cu2+. However, the fluorescence intensity of lanthanide luminescent MOFs is limited by the ligand (fluorescence energy supplier). It causes the unremarkable fluorescence intensity and quantum yield, which indirectly affects the detection sensitivity of the target.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have emerged in the field of fluorescence sensing because of their environmentally friendly, non-toxic and low-cost synthesis [23–25].CDs have high quantum yield, good water solubility and stable fluorescence intensity, which enable efficient detection of specific HMs in water [26–29]. For example, Liu et al [27] synthesized a yellow fluorescent CD for the detection of Hg2+, and Keerthana et al [30] synthesized a CD from water amaranth leaves and combined it with 1-pyrenecarboxaldehyde (PC) to obtain PC-CD, which enables proportional fluorescence detection for Cd2+. In addition, the rich functional group types on the surface of CD make it easy to combine with MOFs to modulate the luminescent properties of the raw MOFs and enhance the sensing effect of the CD-MOF composites. Sonowal et al [22] loaded graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots onto porous Zr-based MOF, which enabled the material to have good fluorescence response for Hg2+. Zhang [31] as well as Yang [32] loaded CD onto ZIF-8, and synthesized CD-MOF fluorescent materials to achieve the detection and analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. These studies show that CD can enrich the specific sites on the MOF surface and enhance the detection performance of the material. Thus, the composites of CD and MOF have great potential for application in fluorescence detection. However, the mechanism by which different HMs lead to various fluorescence responses of materials has not been clearly investigated. In addition, it is worth noting that most of the currently constructed sensing platforms are just for a single HM, which is limited in the application for real water detection where multiple HMs coexist.

To address this problem, we prepared Eu3+-doped luminescent MOF material UiO-66-NH2(Eu), which compounded with sulfur-containing carbon dots CD(s). The doping of CD(s) modulated the luminescent properties of UiO-66-NH2(Eu), and obtaining a dual-emission fluorescent material, UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s). Based on the strong affinity of the element S for the selected targets Hg2+ and Pb2+, we constructed a “turn-on” fluorescence sensing platform, which realized the fluorescence detection of trace Hg2+ and Pb2+ in a wide range of temperature and pH. Furthermore, on the basis of analyzing the different fluorescence response for Hg2+ and Pb2+, we resolved the fluorescence correlation mechanism under the action of the two targets. Accordingly, a three-dimensional decoding map was designed to analyze the concentrations of both Hg2+ and Pb2+ in the coexistence system, and the simultaneous detection in real water samples was achieved. The research results provide new ideas for the simultaneous rapid detection of multiple pollutants in the water environment.

2-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, AR.98.0 %), zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, AR.98.0 %), Europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O, AR.99.9 %), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S·9H2O, AR.99.9 %), citric acid (C6H6O7 (CA), AR ≥99.5 %), L-Cysteine, NaCl (GR.99.8 %), MgCl2 (CP,99 %), KCl (99.8 %), CaCl2 (96 %), FeCl3·6H2O (AR.99 %), Cd(NO3)2·4H2O (AR.99.0 %), Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (AR.99.0 %), Hg2+ solution (100 µg/ml, 5 % HNO3), Pb2+ solution (100 µg/ml, 5 % HNO3) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Reagent. Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (≥97 %), CuCl2 (AR.98.0 %), ZnCl2 (AR,98.0 %) were supplied by Rhawn Reagent. BaCl2 was purchased from Shanghai Shanpu Chemical Reagent. NaOH (AR, 500 g) was provided by Sinopharm chemical reagent and HNO3 (AR, 95 %–98 %) was purchased from Nanjing WANQING Chemical Reagent. N,N'-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR ≥99.5 %), Methanol (AR ≥99.5 %), Ethanol (AR ≥99.7 %) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent.

CD(s) was prepared as follows: 0.384 g CA and 1.44 g Na2S were dissolved in 50 mL water and the mixture was transferred to an autoclave. After the reaction at 200 °C for 4 h, the solid impurities from the reaction were filtered off to obtain the light-yellow solution (blue fluorescent). The solution was then dialyzed (MD55-500 membrane) with pure water for 24 h until its color changed to transparent. The dialyzed carbon dots solution was freeze-dried to obtain white powder solid. Afterwards, the solid carbon dots were dissolved in 20 mL pure water to obtain the purified high-quality CD(s) solution (concentration 5 g/L). The synthesized CD(s) solution was placed at 4 °C for refrigerated storage.

UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) was prepared as follows: taking 0.35 g ZrCl4 (1.5 mM), 0.27 g NH2-BDC (1.5 mM) and 0.055 g EuCl3·6H2O (0.15 mM) in 45 mL DMF and sonicated for 5 min, then added 0.7 mL CD(s) solution to the mixture. Later, the mixture was transferred to the autoclave and reacted at 120 °C for 24 h. After cleaning the solid powder in the reactor with DMF, ethanol, water and methanol solutions, the powder material was activated at 150 °C to remove the residual organic solvents in the MOF pores. Through the above procedures, we obtained orange powder material UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s). The synthesis of other CDs and the optimization of MOFs fabrication are detailed in Texts S1-S4 in Support Information.

The MOF dispersion with uniform concentration (0.5 g/L) was obtained by taking 5 m g UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) added to 10 mL water and sonicated for 3 min. Then, take 2 mL dispersion in a quartz cuvette and added 0–200 μL Hg2+ and Pb2+ solutions/ mixed Hg2+ and Pb2+ solution with different concentrations (0–10 mg/L), respectively. After 2 min, test the change in fluorescence intensity of the dispersion after adding Hg2+ and Pb2+ under the conditions of an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and an output voltage of 400 V (within the wavelength range of 380–760 nm).

Comparison of the detection effect of MOFs, elimination of interfering factors during detection, material selectivity, interference resistance and optimization of detection conditions are shown in Texts S5-S9 in Support Information.

In order to evaluate the practical capability of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) nano-sensor for the detection of Hg2+/Pb2+, we used the standard addition method to monitor Hg2+/Pb2+ in tap water, groundwater, and sewage plant effluent. The tap water was from the water supply pipe in Nanjing, the groundwater was taken from wells in the Pukou district of Nanjing, and the sewage plant water was taken from the treated tail water of a sewage plant in Nanjing. Lead-containing water was taken from seepage water from pits in the suburbs of Nanjing.

Added different concentrations of Hg2+/Pb2+ standard solution samples (the initial indexes of tap water, groundwater, sewage plant effluent and lead-containing water samples are shown in Table S1) in 0.5 g/L material dispersion solution and recorded the changes in fluorescence intensity at 360 nm excitation.
See Text S10 in Support Information for details.

It is well known that sulfur element has a good affinity for mercury and lead. Therefore, we used CA, Na2S as raw materials to synthesize CD(s) emitting blue fluorescence for enhancing the sensitivity of the material for the targets. It was complexed with Eu3+ doped UiO-66-NH2 to obtain the dual-emission fluorescent material UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) with emission peaks at 435 nm and 715 nm. Under the influence of Hg2+, the intensity of both fluorescence peaks of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) increased with its concentration (Fig. 1 a). This trend was opposite to the significant fluorescence quenching based on pure CD(s) [33], which laid the foundation for constructing a “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for Hg2+.

It was worth noting that different synthesizing methods of CD significantly affected the composite properties. We synthesized CD(L-cys) from cysteine (L-cys) and introduced it into UiO-66-NH2 to obtain the composite UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(L-cys). Although UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(L-cys) had the same trend of fluorescence response to Hg2+ (Fig. 1b), the fluorescence was not stable. As shown in Fig. 1c,d, the fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) was not affected by the concentration of the system, whereas UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(L-cys) would undergo fluorescence changes with the decrease of its own concentration.

In addition, the CDs and Eu3+ dosage also affected the detection performance. Figure S1,S2 in Support Information indicated that the composites synthesized by adding 0.7 mL of CD(s) combined the best fluorescence stability and detection sensitivity. The increase of CD(s) (to 1 mL) would significantly affect crystal size and morphological structure of the material (Figure S3 in Support Information). In addition, 1 mL of CD(s) introduced into UiO-66-NH2 severely weakened the dispersion properties of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s), causing it to precipitate and aggregate in water rapidly. Subsequently, based on the optimization of the Eu dose (Fig. S4a,b,c in Support Information), UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) obtained with 0.7 mL CD(s) and 0.15 mM Eu3+ had the best fluorescence stability and the material had the most pronounced fluorescence response to Hg2+ (Fig. S4d in Support Information). Furthermore, the material had excellent water stability within 150 min in water (Figure S5 in Support Information).

SEM and TEM were used to observe the formal characteristics of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 2 a,b), UiO-66-NH2(Eu) (Fig. 2c,d), and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) (Fig. 2e,f). It was found that the introduction of CD(s) significantly changed the morphology of UiO-66-NH2. Many fine granular crystals were grown on the surface of the material, which were proved to be CDs with lattice of about 0.215 nm (Fig. 3 ). Based on EDS and elemental mapping spectra (Figure S6, S7 in Support Information) analysis, the successful combination of sulfur-containing carbon dots and MOF was further demonstrated. Combined with XRD characterization (Figure S8 in Support Information), doped Eu3+ and CD(s) in UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) did not affect the overall crystal diffraction peak positions and crystalline structure. However, the peaks intensity for UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) was lower, indicating a slight decrease in the crystallinity, which was also reflected in the SEM images. In addition, the effect of doped CD(s) and Eu3+ on the apparent texture of the material was explored by BET. According to Table S2, UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) had a specific surface area of 636.9099 m2/g and a pore volume of 0.36 cm3/g, which were similar to those of UiO-66-NH2 in previous public research (specific surface area of 600–800 m2/g and pore volume of ∼0.38 cm3/g for UiO-66-NH2 [34,35]). It further indicated that the doping of CD(s) and Eu3+ basically did not disrupt the pore characteristics. Fig. S9a in Support Information displayed that the N2 adsorption–desorption curve of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) were type IV with narrow hysteresis loops in the range of relative pressures (P/P0) 0.6 ∼ 1.0, which indicated the presence of mesoporous structures in material.

The FTIR characteristic peaks of the UiO-66-NH2, UiO-66-NH2(Eu), UiO-66-NH2-CD(s) and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) were basically the same, suggesting that the incorporation of Eu and CD(s) did not change the original functional group type of UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. S10a in Support Information). Compared with UiO-66-NH2, the absorption peak of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) at 666c m−1 was blue shifted, indicating that the C-S functional group and CD(s) were successfully introduced to the MOF material [36] (Fig. 4 a). Moreover, the absorption peaks at ∼ 1260c m−1 and 3360–3500c m−1 represented for amino groups (Fig. S10b in Support Information), and the 1350–1700c m−1 peaks were attributed to the synergistic effect of amino, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid groups (Fig. S10a in Support Information) [37,38]. TG analysis (Fig. 4b) showed three distinct weight loss intervals (30 ∼ 115/150 °C; 115/150 ∼ 565 °C; 565 ∼ 595/600 °C) for both UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s). The mass loss below 150 °C was caused by the volatilization of guest molecules (water, DMF, etc.). During 115/150 ∼ 280 °C, the material framework was dehydroxylated, but the Zr-O bond was retained. As the raised temperature, the rupture of the linkage between the central metal and the oxygen atoms of the MOF resulted in a further mass loss. When the temperature increased to 565 ∼ 595/600 °C, pyrolysis of the benzene ring lead to the last significant decrease in material mass, after which the TG curve leveled off [39]. In addition, loading of CD(s) reduced the crystallinity of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) and meanwhile increased hydroxyl functional groups on the surface (Figure S11 in Support Information illustrated that UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) had a higher C-O(H) intensity at 1100–1200 cm−1 than UiO-66-NH2), leading to slightly lower thermal stability than UiO-66-NH2 (Fig. 4b) [40].

The differences in fluorescence properties of CD(s), UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) were analyzed using fluorescence spectra and energy calculations. UiO-66-NH2 doped with CD(s) and Eu3+ showed an increase in overall fluorescence energy with two obvious fluorescence emission peaks at 436 nm and 712 nm. By energy calculation (Fig. 5 a), the fluorescence energies of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) at 436 and 712 nm were 6.7 and 3.7 eV, respectively, which were higher than those of UiO-66-NH2. According to Figure S12 in Support Information and Fig. 5a, CD(s) had a strong fluorescence peak at 436 nm. After complexation, part of the fluorescence energy was transferred to Eu3+, which sensitized its emission peak at 712 nm(5D0 → 7F4 leap of Eu3+ [37]) and made the fluorescence energy of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) at 712 nm (3.7 eV) significantly larger than CD(s) (1.4 eV) and UiO-66-NH2 (1.1 eV). Further observation of the fluorescence emission spectra revealed that UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) appeared emission peaks at 593, 616 (Fig. 5b), which corresponded to the characteristic 5D0 → 7F J (J=1,2) leap of Eu3+ [8]. Combining the UV–Vis spectra (Figure S13 in Support Information), Eu3+ and carbon dots basically did not affect the emission peak positions of the material. In addition, the composites introducing CD(s) and Eu3+ had better detection sensitivity for Hg2+ (Figure S14 in Support Information).

CD(s) exhibited fluorescence quenching when exposed to Hg2+ [33]. However, the composite synthesized in this work displayed significant fluorescence enhancement in the presence of Hg2+. In order to clarify the above differences in depth, we observed the TEM images of CD(s) after interacting with Hg2+. CD(s) underwent aggregation upon contact with Hg2+ (Figure S15a,b in Support Information). However, the morphology and dispersibility of the crystal particles did not change significantly (Figure S16c,d in Support Information). According to a previous study [29], the fluorescence extinction of CD(s) was due to the complexation of Hg2+ with the sulfur-containing group and carboxyl group in the carbon dots, which resulted the aggregation of CD(s). This process increased the non-radiative leaps of electrons and decreased the fluorescence intensity. In contrast, UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) would coordinate with Hg2+ and trigger the chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) mechanism. Therefore, the fluorescence extinction caused of CD(s) aggregation was clearly different from the CHEF between UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) and Hg2+, so that the composites was an excellent carrier for “turn-on” sensing platform.

The fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) at 436 and 712 nm gradually increased with the Hg2+ concentration (0–1 mg/L) (Fig. 6 a,b) with a very good linear relationship (R2 of 0.99867 and 0.99245, respectively, Figure S16). Meanwhile, the material had good selectivity and anti-interference properties (Fig. 6c,d). Different from the response under of multiple impurities, Hg2+ resulted in a more substantial enhancement of the material fluorescence. In addition, it was found that low concentration Pb2+ also caused strong changes in fluorescence (Fig. 6e,f). Figure S17 in Support Information showed the increase of Pb2+ led to a significant increase in the fluorescence intensity at 436 nm. Meanwhile, the addition of Hg2+/Pb2+ also caused a change in the dispersion color of the material and the coordinates in the CIE diagram (Figure S18 in Support Information).

The effects of dispersant concentration, dispersant solvent, detection time, pH and temperature on the detection of HMs by UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) were investigated. It could be seen (Figure S19a,b in Support Information) that 0.5 g/L dispersion (water as dispersing agent) was the optimal environment for detection. Simultaneously, Hg2+/Pb2+ could lead to a rapid increase of material fluorescence intensity within 1 min and stabilized at 2 min (Figure S20 in Support Information). In addition, Figure S21 in Support Information showed that UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) had a good detection of Hg2+/Pb2+ in the range of temperature 15–35 °C and pH = 4–7. Compared to CD(s) (optimum pH of 6–7 [33]), the composite exhibited a wider optimum pH range (4–7). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity and crystal structure were largely unaffected under acidic and neutral conditions. (Figure S22,23 in Support Information). These results illustrated that UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) could be adapted to more complex practical environments. Based on the above, we selected 2 min reaction time, 20 °C and pH=7 as the detection conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence detection for trace Hg2+/Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the fluorescence properties, we constructed a fluorescence sensing platform to detect trace Hg2+ and Pb2+ in water, respectively. A good linear relationship between the emission peak intensity at 436 nm and the target concentration (0–100 μg/L) was calculated (R2 Hg = 0.9793, R2 Pb = 0.99479, Figure S24,25 in Support Information), and the theoretical detection limits were LODHg = 15.38 μg/L (76.67 nM) and LODPb = 1.06 μg/L (5.12 nM), respectively (Figure S26,27 in Support Information, Table S3). The material had ultra-sensitive fluorescence responses to trace Hg2+ and Pb2+ (the above formula and results were shown in Text S11 of the Supporting Information), and the sensitivities were higher than most of the reported researches (Table S4, Table S5). Regarding the limit of quantitation (LOQ), we combined Fig. 1c,d to find that the change of dispersion concentration had a large effect on the material fluorescence when the ratio of the fluorescence intensity after detection to the initial fluorescence intensity (I/I0) was less than 1.16. Therefore, to ensure a true and reliable detection, the LOQ of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) for Hg2+ and Pb2+ was determined to be 30 μg/L and 10 μg/L, respectively (Figure S24c, Figure S28 in Support Information).

After that, we added the prepared Hg2+/Pb2+-containing actual water samples into the UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) dispersion to check the actual detection effect of the material. As shown in Table S6, S7, the recoveries of Hg2+/Pb2+ for the actual spiked water samples were in the range of 93.15 ∼ 105.08 % with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) in the range of 0.17 % ∼ 0.92 %. The effectiveness of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) for the direct detection of trace lead containing groundwater samples was also tested (Table S8). The above results indicated the feasibility of this fluorescence sensing platform for the detection of a single Hg2+/Pb2+.

Based on these results, we analyzed the mechanism of action between UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) and Hg2+/Pb2+. From the FTIR characterization (Figure S29 in Support Information), the characteristic peaks of amino group, carboxylic acid group and C-S (mainly –NH2 and sulfur-containing functional groups) were significantly weakened after the interaction of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) with Hg2+/Pb2+, indicating that the material interacted with Hg2+/Pb2+. XPS characterization revealed that the composites showed distinct peaks of Hg4f and Pb4f after detection of Hg2+/Pb2+ (Figure S30 in Support Information). Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in Fig. 7 a,b corresponded to binding energies of 100.91 (Hg4f7/2), 104.95 eV (Hg4f5/2) and 138.06 (Pb4f7/2) and 142.99 eV (Pb4f5/2), respectively, confirmed the interaction between Hg2+/Pb2+ and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s). Combined with the XPS spectra of C, O, and Zr (Figure S31a,b,c in Support Information), the peak positions of O, and Zr elements before and after the detection of Hg2+/Pb2+ remained basically unchanged, which demonstrated that the Zr-O bonding connection for material did not be affected. However, different with unchanged C1s spectra (C-C, Figure S31a in Support Information), the O-C=O of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) went from 288.16 eV→288.02 eV after the Pb2+ detection, while no change of that after Hg2+ action. This discrepancy suggested a possible weak interaction between Pb2+ and the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the material [41,42]. Besides, the N1s,S2p spectrum of the composites (Figure S32a in Support Information) illustrated that the binding energy of –NH2 peak was reduced after the detection of Hg2+/Pb2+. The more positively charged N in the compound, the higher electronic binding energy it has [43]. Therefore, the decrease in the binding energy of N1s (398.76 eV → 398.66/398.65 eV) indicated the coordination of Hg2+/Pb2+ with the amino functional groups for the material [44]. After detection of Hg2+/Pb2+, the binding energies of the S2p peaks all decreased compared with the initial, indicating S elements in the material interacted with Hg2+/Pb2+ [45]. Furthermore, two new peaks appeared in the S2p spectra of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s)@Hg and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s)@Pb (Figure S32b in Support Information). This change was caused by the creation of a new complex between the S-containing groups and Hg2+/Pb2+ [46] (Fig. 8 ). The N2 adsorption–desorption and BJH curves (Figure S9) illustrated the apparent textural properties and pore size distributions of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) were basically unchanged after the detection of Hg2+/Pb2+ (Figure S9). However, compared with Hg2+, a significant decrease in the specific surface area (636.91 m2/g → 478.69 m2/g) and pore volume (0.3640 cm3/g → 0.3149 cm3/g) occurred after interaction with Pb2+ (Table S2). It might suggest the different adsorption sites between Hg2+ and Pb2+, and this discrepancy was also reflected in the change of binding energies for N1s, C1s, and S2p spectrums.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in mixed systems
ce:section.ce:para: According to Fig. 6c, Hg2+ enhanced both dual emission peaks (436 and 712 nm) of the material (Fig. 6b), on the contrary, Pb2+ only enhanced the 436 nm emission peaks dramatically (Fig. 6f). Accordingly, a method for simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ was explored.

First, we examined the effect of different concentration ratios of Hg2+ and Pb2+ coexistence system (c(Hg2+):c(Pb2+) = 0:1, 0.25:1, 0.5:1, 1:1, 1:0.5, 1:0.25, 1:0) on the fluorescence intensity of the materials. As shown in Figure S33a,b in Support Information, the more Pb2+ in the mixed system, the larger fluorescence intensity increments I (436 nm)/I0 (436 nm), whereas I (712 nm)/I0 (712 nm) was almost unchanged. Nevertheless, I (712 nm)/I0 (712 nm) increased significantly as the percentage of Hg2+ increased in the mixed system. Based on the different response types (Figure S33c,d, Figure S34b in Support Information), we constructed a three-dimensional data model between concentration and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 9 a) to realize the simultaneous analysis of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in the coexistence system.

As shown in Fig. 9b, as the of Pb2+ percentage in the mixed system increased, the 3D concentration-fluorescence intensity curve in the model would gradually shift from the blue curve to the left green curve, and then moved to the purple one. Accordingly, the simultaneous detection of two HMs can be realized. After introducing the fluorescence changes at 436 or 712 nm as coordinates (I (436nm)/I0 (436nm), I (712nm)/I0 (712nm)) on the x-y plane projection of the 3D model, the concentration values (concentrations marked red in Fig. 9a) of Z-axis would be found in 3D model and the respective concentrations of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in the samples would be obtained. The above method could realize the simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in the concentration range of 400–1000 μg/L. Although the accuracy of this method in the lower concentration of Hg2+ and Pb2+ for mixed systems required improvement (Figure S35 in Support Information), it provided a theoretical guide for the simultaneous detection of multiple metal ions in mixed system (Figure S36 in Support Information for the original data graph of the experiment).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the simultaneous detection model in real water

ce:section.ce:para: FTIR (Figure S29 in Support Information) and XPS characterization results (Figure S32 in Support Information) confirmed that Hg2+/Pb2+ would coordinate with the amino functional group in UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) and CD(s), which caused the CHEF effect and induced fluorescence enhancement [47–50]. Meanwhile, the CD(s) contained in UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) enhanced the complexation of Hg2+/Pb2+, which resulted in better fluorescence sensing performance. The CHEF effect was exhibited macroscopically as the Hg2+/Pb2+ introduction resulted in a significant fluorescence enhancement at 436 nm.

It was well known that lanthanide luminescence mainly relied on the “antenna effect”, and the efficiency of ligand energy transfer to rare metals dominated the material fluorescence intensity [51]. And it is worth noting that some transition metal ions could improve this transfer efficiency [52–55]. In addition, complexation of different metal ion guests with functional groups in the MOF channel would disturb the electronic structure of the ligand with varying degrees. It affected the single-linear and triplet excited states of the fluorescent ligand, which would in turn change the energy transfer efficiency of the lanthanide metal [56]. Mercury, as a transition element, improved the fluorescence energy transfer efficiency of the ligand to Eu3+ in UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s), and the 712 nm peak (Eu3+ characteristic emission peak) increased isometrically with the 436 nm one. On the contrary, the non-transitional Pb2+ did not enhance the “antenna effect”. It could not trigger energy transferring to Eu with just single-peak enhancement at 436 nm. DFT calculation further verified the above speculations. Fig. 11 indicated that the fluorescence energies of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) at 436 and 712 nm changed from the initial 6.7 and 3.7 eV to 7.5 and 6.1 eV after the detection of Hg2+, suggesting that the energy of two fluorescence emission peaks increased after Hg2+ interaction. However, Pb2+ only made the 436 nm energy increase from 6.7 eV to 8.8 eV. Accordingly, we constructed a “turn-on” fluorescent probe that could realize the simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ by using the above discrepancy in fluorescence signals and energy.

In order to deeply understand the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of Hg2+/Pb2+ on the material, we analyzed the UV–vis spectra of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s), UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s)@Hg and UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s)@Pb. It could be found that Hg2+/Pb2+ decreased the absorption peak of UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) (Figure S37 in Support Information). Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime was also changed. As shown in Fig. 12 and Figure S38 in Support Information, as well as the fitting results of Table S9, the fluorescence lifetimes of at 436 and 712 nm reduced significantly compared to the initial material (436 nm: 0.44 → 0.33 ns, 712 nm: 16.07 → 9.97 ns), which was in sharp contrast to the change induced by Pb2+ (in particular for the emission peak at 436 nm, the average lifetime decreased by only 0.04 ns). All these were attributed to Hg2+ induced fluorescence energy transfer within the MOF material [57]. The fitted analysis reconfirmed the conclusions drawn from previous XPS, FTIR mapping and provided practical proof of our hypothesized fluorescence sensing mechanism.

In this study, we reported an Eu-doped CD-MOF composite UiO-66-NH2(Eu)-CD(s) with dual-emission fluorescence for “turn-on” detection of trace Hg2+ and Pb2+ in the aqueous environment. The N, S groups of the material were coordinated with the targets and produced CHEF effect, which realized the selective sensing with enhanced sensitivity. The materials were able to quantify Hg2+ and Pb2+ rapidly over a wide range of concentrations (0–1000 μg/L), pH conditions (4–7), and temperatures (15–35 °C), with detection limits as low as 76.67 nM for Hg2+ and 5.12 nM for Pb2+ within 2 min. Furthermore, Hg2+ promoted the fluorescence energy transfer of the material at the 436 → 712 nm emission peak, while Pb2+ did not. Based on this different fluorescence signal changes, a three-dimensional data model was constructed to realize the simultaneous detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in actual water samples. The recoveries of Hg2+ and Pb2+ in real water ranged from 93.15 % to 105.08 %.
